title: Downloading Projects
subtitle: Getting code from GitHub

sections.content: **Cloning** downloads an entire repository from GitHub to your computer, and **pulling** fetches the latest changes from a repo you already have. These are how you get code from others.
sections.analogy: Cloning is like downloading someone's entire photo album to your computer — you get the full history. Pulling is like checking for new photos that were added since your last download. Clone once, then pull to stay up to date.
keyPoints: `git clone URL` downloads a full repository including all history
`git pull` fetches and merges the latest changes from the remote
Clone is a one-time setup; pull is something you do regularly

sections.instruction: Most developers start by downloading an existing project from GitHub. `git clone` copies the entire project — all files and their full history.

onSuccess.message: The project is downloaded with its full history! A new folder called `todo-app` was created. This is how you start working on any existing project.

sections.instruction: When other people push changes to GitHub, you need to download their updates. `git pull` fetches the latest changes and merges them into your copy.
sections.prompt: Type `git pull` to check for updates.

onSuccess.message: Already up to date. In a real project with teammates, this would download any new commits they pushed. You'll run `git pull` at the start of every work session.

completionMessage: `git clone` downloads a project for the first time. `git pull` gets updates after that. Together with `git push`, these three commands are how code flows between your computer and GitHub.
